aWeek 11 & 12
1. Probability Density Function - Geometric Perspective 2. Area & Determinant 3. Jacobian Matrix 4. Paralleogram 5. Functions of Random Variable - Discrete 6. Functions of Random Variable - Continuous 7. Transformation of Random Variables (one variable) 8. Transformation of Random Variables (two variable) 9. Bivariate Gaussian Distribution 10. Multivariate Gaussian Distribution 11. Bivariate Gaussian (Plot) 12. Maximum Likelihood 13. Jensen's Inequality 14. Gaussian Mixture Model 15. Concentration Inequalites 16. Convergence in Probability and Convergence Distribution 17. Weak Law of Large Numbers 18. Central Limit Theorem
1. Probability Density Function - Geometric PerspectiveWe have already seen what a PDF is, in the recap of Probability. In this section, we shall see the geometric perspective of it. Let XX be a continuous random variable with fXfX as its PDF. For a sufficiently small value Δx>0Δx>0, we would like to compute the following.P(x⩽X⩽x+Δx)P(x⩽X⩽x+Δx) We would like to find the probability of XX taking a value between xx and x+Δxx+Δx. Here is the usual way,x+Δx∫xfX(x) dx∫xx+ΔxfX(x)dx Basically, the probability that we wish to compute is the area under the curve fX(x)fX(x) between the interval [x,x+Δx][x,x+Δx]. Since ΔxΔx is small, this region can be approximated by a rectangle.
fX(x)xx+Δx For small ΔxΔx, we can approximate the probability as followsP(x⩽X⩽x+Δx)≈fX(x)·ΔxP(x⩽X⩽x+Δx)≈fX(x)·Δx The height of the rectangle is fX(x)fX(x) and the width of the rectangle is ΔxΔx. Similarly, if PP and QQ are two continuous random variables with fPQ(p,q)fPQ(p,q) as the joint PDF, then for sufficiently small Δp>0Δp>0 and Δq>0Δq>0P(p⩽P⩽p+Δp,q⩽Q⩽q+Δq)≈fPQ(p,q)·Δp·ΔqP(p⩽P⩽p+Δp,q⩽Q⩽q+Δq)≈fPQ(p,q)·Δp·Δq Here, the probability is approximately equal to the volume of a thin cuboid whose length is ΔpΔp, width is ΔqΔq and height is fPQ(p,q)fPQ(p,q).
2. Area & DeterminantVectors uu and vv are given as follows.u=a
3
0
, v=a
1
2
u=(30),v=(12) Let the vertices of a parallelogram be (0,0)(0,0), uu, vv and u+vu+v
The are of the paralleogram isBase×HeightBase×Height In the given paralleogram, the height is 22, and the base length is 33. Therefore, the area is 66 units. Consider the following matrixA=a
||
uv
||
=a
31
02
A=[||uv||]=[3102] The absolute value of the determinant of AA is also 66|det(A)|=6|det(A)|=6 If we interchange the column, the absolute value of the deteminant remains the sameadet(a
13
20
)a=6|det([1320])|=6 Given two vectors u,v∈R2u,v∈R2, the area of the parallelogram with vertices (0,0),u,v(0,0),u,v and u+vu+v is the absolute value of the determinant of the 2×22×2 matrix that has uu and vv as its column vectors.
3. Jacobian MatrixSuppose, f:Rd→Rkf:Rd→Rkf(x1,...,xd)=a
g1(x1,...,xd)
⋮
gk(x1,...,xd)
f(x1,...,xd)=(g1(x1,...,xd)⋮gk(x1,...,xd)) Here, gi:Rd→Rgi:Rd→R, ∀i∈{1,...,k}∀i∈{1,...,k} The Jacobian of ff is given as follows.Jf(x,y,z)=a
∇g1(x1,...,xd)T
⋮
∇gk(x1,...,xd)T
Jf(x,y,z)=(∇g1(x1,...,xd)T⋮∇gk(x1,...,xd)T) ∇gi(x,y,z)∇gi(x,y,z) is assumed to be a column vector ∀i∈{1,...,k}∀i∈{1,...,k}. EXAMPLEf(x,y,z)=a
x+y+z
x2+y2+z2
f(x,y,z)=(x+y+zx2+y2+z2)The Jacobian is given as follows.Jf(x,y,z)=a
111
2x2y2z
Jf(x,y,z)=[1112x2y2z]
4. ParalleogramWe will state and prove two facts about parallelogram, which we will use in a later section.Part 1Suppose, there are three vectors a,b,c∈R2a,b,c∈R2, then the quadrilateral joining the following points form a parallelogram.a, a+b, a+c, a+b+ca,a+b,a+c,a+b+c NOTE : Here, the following points should be adjacent.• aa and a+ba+b• aa and a+ca+c• a+ba+b and a+b+ca+b+c• a+ca+c and a+b+ca+b+c The following points should be opposite to each other• a+ba+b and a+ca+c• aa and a+b+ca+b+c Proof :Let us find the squared length of the the sides.• aa and a+ba+b
||(a+b)-(a)||2=bTb
||(a+b)-(a)||2=bTb• aa and a+ca+c
||(a+c)-(a)||2=cTc
||(a+c)-(a)||2=cTc• a+ba+b and a+b+ca+b+c
||(a+b+c)-(a+b)||2=cTc
||(a+b+c)-(a+b)||2=cTc• a+ca+c and a+b+ca+b+c
||(a+b+c)-(a+c)||2=bTb
||(a+b+c)-(a+c)||2=bTb The side joing the vertices aa and a+ba+b and the side joining the vertices a+ca+c and a+b+ca+b+c are the opposite sides of the quadrilateral. The squared length of both the sides is bTbbTb Similarly, the side joining the vertices aa and a+ca+c and the side joining the vertices a+ba+b and a+b+ca+b+c are the opposite sides of the quadrilateral. The squared length of both the sides is cTccTc If the length of the opposite sides of a quadrilateral are equal, then the quadrilateral is a parallelogram. Part 2Consider two vectors pp and qq where, for some 𝛽∈[0,1]𝛽∈[0,1] and some 𝜆∈[0,1]𝜆∈[0,1]p=𝛽bq=𝜆cp=𝛽bq=𝜆c To Prove : The point a+p+qa+p+q lies within the parallelogram formed in Part 1 Proof : Here is a geometric proof.
bbaaqqccppa+pa+pa+qa+qa+ba+ba+ca+ca+b+ca+b+ca+p+qa+p+q We have made use of the parallelogram law of addition. Clearly, a+p+qa+p+q lies with the red parallelogram.
5. Functions of Random Variable - DiscreteXX is a random variable with TT as its range and fXfX as its PMF. Consider a function g:T→Rg:T→R. ThenY=g(X)Y=g(X)can be seen as a composition of two functions.• X:S→TX:S→T, where SS is a the sample space• g:T→Rg:T→R• Therefore, Y:S→RY:S→R is a random variable. Here is a way to find the PMF of YY. Let A={t∈T|g(t)=y}A={t∈T|g(t)=y}
fY(y)=P(Y=y)
=P(g(X)=y)
=P(X∈A)
= ∑t∈AfX(t)
fY(y)=P(Y=y)=P(g(X)=y)=P(X∈A)=∑t∈AfX(t)EXAMPLELet XX be a random variable with T={-2,-1,0,1,2}T={-2,-1,0,1,2} as its range. The PMF of XX is given as follows.
fX(-2)=0.3
fX(-1)=0.1
fX(0)=0.2
fX(1)=0.25
fX(2)=0.15
fX(-2)=0.3fX(-1)=0.1fX(0)=0.2fX(1)=0.25fX(2)=0.15Find the PMF of YY, where Y=g(X)=X2Y=g(X)=X2. For each element in t∈Tt∈T, let us find g(t)g(t)
g(-2)=4
g(-1)=1
g(0)=0
g(1)=1
g(2)=4
g(-2)=4g(-1)=1g(0)=0g(1)=1g(2)=4 YY can take the following values :: {0,1,4}{0,1,4}• {t∈Tag(t)=0}={0}{t∈T|g(t)=0}={0}• {t∈Tag(t)=1}={-1,1}{t∈T|g(t)=1}={-1,1}• {t∈Tag(t)=4}={-2,2}{t∈T|g(t)=4}={-2,2}
fY(0)=P(Y=0)
=P(g(X)=0)
=P({0})
=0.2
fY(0)=P(Y=0)=P(g(X)=0)=P({0})=0.2
fY(1)=P(Y=1)
=P(g(X)=1)
=P({-1,1})
=0.35
fY(1)=P(Y=1)=P(g(X)=1)=P({-1,1})=0.35
fY(4)=P(Y=4)
=P(g(X)=4)
=P({-2,2})
=0.45
fY(4)=P(Y=4)=P(g(X)=4)=P({-2,2})=0.45Sum of Two Random VariablesXX and YY are two discrete random variables with fXYfXY as their joint PMF and TXTX and TYTY as their corresponding range sets. Random variable ZZ is given as followsZ=X+YZ=X+YThe PMF of ZZ is given as follows
fZ(z)=P(Z=z)
=P(X+Y=z)
= ∑t∈TXP(X=t,Y=z-t)
= ∑t∈TXfXY(t,z-t)
fZ(z)=P(Z=z)=P(X+Y=z)=∑t∈TXP(X=t,Y=z-t)=∑t∈TXfXY(t,z-t)If XX and YY are independent, thenfZ(z)= ∑t∈TXfX(t)·fY(z-t)fZ(z)=∑t∈TXfX(t)·fY(z-t)This is called the convolution operation. EXAMPLE X ~ Poisson(𝜆1)X~Poisson(𝜆1) and Y ~ Poission(𝜆2)Y~Poission(𝜆2). XX and YY are independent. Find the PMF of Z=X+YZ=X+Y The range of XX is {0,1,2,...}{0,1,2,...}
fZ(z)=∞∑t=0fX(t)·fY(z-t)
=∞∑t=0e-𝜆1𝜆t1
t!×e-𝜆2𝜆z-t2
(z-t)!
=∞∑t=0e-(𝜆1+𝜆2)·𝜆t1·𝜆z-t2
t!·(z-t)!
=e-(𝜆1+𝜆2)∞∑t=0𝜆t1·𝜆z-t2
t!·(z-t)!
=e-(𝜆1+𝜆2)
z!∞∑t=0z!·𝜆t1·𝜆z-t2
t!·(z-t)!
=e-(𝜆1+𝜆2)
z!∞∑t=0a

·𝜆t1·𝜆z-t2
=e-(𝜆1+𝜆2)·(𝜆1+𝜆2)z
z!
fZ(z)=∑t=0∞fX(t)·fY(z-t)=∑t=0∞e-𝜆1𝜆1tt!×e-𝜆2𝜆2z-t(z-t)!=∑t=0∞e-(𝜆1+𝜆2)·𝜆1t·𝜆2z-tt!·(z-t)!=e-(𝜆1+𝜆2)∑t=0∞𝜆1t·𝜆2z-tt!·(z-t)!=e-(𝜆1+𝜆2)z!∑t=0∞z!·𝜆1t·𝜆2z-tt!·(z-t)!=e-(𝜆1+𝜆2)z!∑t=0∞(zt)·𝜆1t·𝜆2z-t=e-(𝜆1+𝜆2)·(𝜆1+𝜆2)zz!Here, we have used the binomial theormz∑t=0a

Hence, Z ~ Poisson(𝜆1+𝜆2)Z~Poisson(𝜆1+𝜆2) Minimum of Two Random VariablesXX and YY are two discrete random variables with joint PMF fXYfXY, FXFX and FYFY as their corresponding CDF and TXTX and TYTY as their corresponding range sets. Z=min(X,Y)Z=min(X,Y)Find the PMF and CDF of ZZ PMF of ZZ
fZ(z)=P(Z=Z)
=P(min(X,Y)=z)
=P(X=z,Y=Z)+P(X=z,Y>z)+P(X>z,Y=z)
=fXY(z,z)+ ∑t∈ay∈Tyay>zafXY(z,t)+ ∑t∈ax∈Txax>zafXY(t,z)
fZ(z)=P(Z=Z)=P(min(X,Y)=z)=P(X=z,Y=Z)+P(X=z,Y>z)+P(X>z,Y=z)=fXY(z,z)+∑t∈{y∈Ty|y>z}fXY(z,t)+∑t∈{x∈Tx|x>z}fXY(t,z)CDF of ZZ
FZ(z)=P(Z⩽z)
=P(min(X,Y)⩽z)
=1-P(min(X,Y)>z)
=1-P(X>z,Y>z)
FZ(z)=P(Z⩽z)=P(min(X,Y)⩽z)=1-P(min(X,Y)>z)=1-P(X>z,Y>z)If XX and YY are independent, then,
FZ(z)=1-P(X>z,Y>z)
=1-[P(X>z)·P(Y>z)]
=1-[(1-P(X⩽z)·(1-P(Y⩽z)]
=1-(1-FX(z))·(1-FY(z))
FZ(z)=1-P(X>z,Y>z)=1-[P(X>z)·P(Y>z)]=1-[(1-P(X⩽z)·(1-P(Y⩽z)]=1-(1-FX(z))·(1-FY(z))Maximum of Two Random VariablesXX and YY are two discrete random variables with joint PDF fXYfXY, FXFX and FYFY as their corresponding CDF and TXTX and TYTY as their corresponding range sets. Z=max(X,Y)Z=max(X,Y)Find the PDF and CDF of ZZ PMF of ZZ
fZ(z)=P(Z=Z)
=P(max(X,Y)=z)
=P(X=z,Y=Z)+P(X=z,Y<z)+P(X<z,Y=z)
=fXY(z,z)+ ∑t∈ay∈Tyay<zafXY(z,t)+ ∑t∈ax∈Txax<zafXY(t,z)
fZ(z)=P(Z=Z)=P(max(X,Y)=z)=P(X=z,Y=Z)+P(X=z,Y<z)+P(X<z,Y=z)=fXY(z,z)+∑t∈{y∈Ty|y<z}fXY(z,t)+∑t∈{x∈Tx|x<z}fXY(t,z)CDF of ZZ
FZ(z)=P(Z⩽z)
=P(max(X,Y)⩽z)
=P(X⩽z,Y⩽z)
FZ(z)=P(Z⩽z)=P(max(X,Y)⩽z)=P(X⩽z,Y⩽z) If XX and YY are independent, then,
FZ(z)=P(X⩽z,Y⩽z)
=P(X⩽z)·P(Y⩽z)
=FX(z)·FY(z)
FZ(z)=P(X⩽z,Y⩽z)=P(X⩽z)·P(Y⩽z)=FX(z)·FY(z) ExpectationXX is a discrete random variable with fXfX as its PMF and TXTX as its range. Then,E[g(X)]= ∑x∈TXg(x)·fX(x)E[g(X)]=∑x∈TXg(x)·fX(x)
6. Functions of Random Variable - ContinuousXX is a continuous random variable with CDF FXFX and PDF fXfX. Suppose, Y=g(X)Y=g(X). Here, gg should be a reasonable function. The precise meaning of a reasonable function is beyond the scope of this course. For the purposes of this course, any function we encounter is considered reasonable. Let A={x|g(x)⩽y}A={x|g(x)⩽y}. The CDF of YY is given as followsa
FY(y)=P(Y⩽y)
=P(g(X)⩽y)
=P(X∈A)
FY(y)=P(Y⩽y)=P(g(X)⩽y)=P(X∈A) First, we shall convert the set AA into disjoint intervals.A=[a1,b1]∪...∪[an,bn]A=[a1,b1]∪...∪[an,bn] Then,
FY(y)=P(X∈A)
=P(a1⩽X⩽b1)+...+P(an⩽X⩽bn)
=n∑i=1bi∫aifX(x) dx
FY(y)=P(X∈A)=P(a1⩽X⩽b1)+...+P(an⩽X⩽bn)=∑i=1n∫aibifX(x)dx EXAMPLEConsider X ~ Exponential(𝜆)X~Exponential(𝜆). Find the CDF of Y=g(X)=X2Y=g(X)=X2 Let y⩾0y⩾0 and let A={x|g(x)⩽y}A={x|g(x)⩽y}
A={x|g(x)⩽y)
={x|x2⩽y}
=[0,y]
A={x|g(x)⩽y)={x|x2⩽y}=[0,y]
FY(y)=y∫0𝜆e-𝜆x dx
=𝜆y∫0e-𝜆x dx
=𝜆[e-𝜆x
-𝜆]y0
=𝜆[e-𝜆y-1
-𝜆]
=1-e-𝜆y
FY(y)=∫0y𝜆e-𝜆xdx=𝜆∫0ye-𝜆xdx=𝜆[e-𝜆x-𝜆]0y=𝜆[e-𝜆y-1-𝜆]=1-e-𝜆y Sum of Two Random VariablesXX and YY are two continuous random variables with fXYfXY as their joint PDF. Random variable ZZ is given as followsZ=X+YZ=X+YThe PDF of ZZ is given as follows
fZ(z)=∞∫-∞fXY(x,z-x) dx
fZ(z)=∫-∞∞fXY(x,z-x)dxIf XX and YY are independent, thenfZ(z)=∞∫-∞fX(x)·fY(z-x) dxfZ(z)=∫-∞∞fX(x)·fY(z-x)dxThis is called the convolution operation. Minimum of Two Random VariablesXX and YY are two continuous random variables with joint PDF fXYfXY. Let FXFX and FYFY be their corresponding CDFs. Z=min(X,Y)Z=min(X,Y). Find the CDF of ZZCDF of ZZ
FZ(z)=P(Z⩽z)
=P(min(X,Y)⩽z)
=1-P(min(X,Y)>z)
=1-P(X>z,Y>z)
=1-∞∫z∞∫zfXY(x,y) dy dx
FZ(z)=P(Z⩽z)=P(min(X,Y)⩽z)=1-P(min(X,Y)>z)=1-P(X>z,Y>z)=1-∫z∞∫z∞fXY(x,y)dydx If XX and YY are independent, thena
FZ(z)=1-P(X>z,Y>z)
=1-(P(X>z)·P(Y>z))
=1-(1-P(X⩽z))·(1-P(Y⩽z))
=1-(1-FX(x))·(1-FY(y))
FZ(z)=1-P(X>z,Y>z)=1-(P(X>z)·P(Y>z))=1-(1-P(X⩽z))·(1-P(Y⩽z))=1-(1-FX(x))·(1-FY(y)) Maximum of Two Random VariablesXX and YY are two continuous random variables with joint PDF fXYfXY. Let FXFX and FYFY be their corresponding CDFs. Z=max(X,Y)Z=max(X,Y). Find the CDF of ZZCDF of ZZ
FZ(z)=P(Z⩽z)
=P(max(X,Y)⩽z)
=P(X⩽z,Y⩽z)
=z∫-∞z∫-∞fXY(x,y) dy dx
FZ(z)=P(Z⩽z)=P(max(X,Y)⩽z)=P(X⩽z,Y⩽z)=∫-∞z∫-∞zfXY(x,y)dydx If XX and YY are independent, thena
FZ(z)=P(X⩽z,Y⩽z)
=P(X⩽z)·P(Y⩽z)
=FX(z)·FY(z)
FZ(z)=P(X⩽z,Y⩽z)=P(X⩽z)·P(Y⩽z)=FX(z)·FY(z) ExpectationXX is a continuous random variable with fXfX as its PDF. Then,E[g(X)]=∞∫-∞g(x)·fX(x) dxE[g(X)]=∫-∞∞g(x)·fX(x)dx
7. Transformation of Random Variables (one variable)Part 1 Let XX be a continuous random variable with fXfX as its PDF. The random variable YY is given as followsY=g(X)Y=g(X) where, gg is a differentiable, invertible & strictly increasing function. That is,X=g-1(Y)X=g-1(Y)and for all x1x1 and x2x2 in the domain of gg, if x1<x2x1<x2g(x1)<g(x2)g(x1)<g(x2) Also, for all xx in the domain of gg, g'(x)>0g'(x)>0 GOAL : To find fYfY, which is the PDF of YY. For a sufficiently small Δx>0Δx>0,P(x⩽X⩽x+Δx)≈fX(x)·ΔxP(x⩽X⩽x+Δx)≈fX(x)·Δx y=g(x)y+Δy=g(x+Δx)y=g(x)y+Δy=g(x+Δx) Here, ΔyΔy is need not be equal to ΔxΔx. The quantity ΔyΔy denotes the change in yy corresponding to change in xx. Consider the derivative of g(x)g(x)g'(x)=limΔx→0g(x+Δx)-g(x)
Δxg'(x)=limΔx→0g(x+Δx)-g(x)Δx Since g(x+Δx)=y+Δyg(x+Δx)=y+Δy and g(x)=yg(x)=y, we getg'(x)=limΔx→0Δy
Δxg'(x)=limΔx→0ΔyΔx As Δx→0Δx→0Δy
Δx→g'(x)ΔyΔx→g'(x) Therefore, for sufficiently small ΔxΔx,Δy≈g'(x)·ΔxΔy≈g'(x)·Δx As Δx→0Δx→0• x+Δx→xx+Δx→x• g(x+Δx)→g(x)g(x+Δx)→g(x)• y+Δy→yy+Δy→y• Δy=(y+Δy)-y→0Δy=(y+Δy)-y→0 Since gg is an increasing function, g(x)<g(x+Δx)g(x)<g(x+Δx). Therefore, Δy=g(x+Δx)-g(x)Δy=g(x+Δx)-g(x) is positive. Therefore,P(y⩽Y⩽y+Δy)≈fY(y)·ΔyP(y⩽Y⩽y+Δy)≈fY(y)·Δy ClaimP(y⩽Y⩽y+Δy)=P(x⩽X⩽x+Δx)P(y⩽Y⩽y+Δy)=P(x⩽X⩽x+Δx) Proof :Consider the eventy⩽Y⩽y+Δyy⩽Y⩽y+Δy Substitute Y=g(X)Y=g(X)y⩽g(X)⩽y+Δyy⩽g(X)⩽y+Δy Since y=g(x)y=g(x) and y+Δy=g(x+Δx)y+Δy=g(x+Δx), we getg(x)⩽g(X)⩽g(x+Δx)g(x)⩽g(X)⩽g(x+Δx) Since gg is invertible and stricly increasingg(x)⩽g(X)⩽g(x+Δx)g(x)⩽g(X)⩽g(x+Δx)impliesx⩽X⩽x+Δxx⩽X⩽x+Δx For all x∈[x,x+Δx]x^∈[x,x+Δx], g(x)∈ag(x),g(x+Δx)ag(x^)∈[g(x),g(x+Δx)]. Therefore,P(y⩽Y⩽y+Δy)=P(x⩽X⩽x+Δx)P(y⩽Y⩽y+Δy)=P(x⩽X⩽x+Δx) Recall Δy≈g'(x)·ΔxΔy≈g'(x)·Δx
P(y⩽Y⩽y+Δy)=P(x⩽X⩽x+Δx)
fy(y)·Δy=fX(x)·Δx
fy(y)·g'(x)·Δx=fX(x)·Δx
fy(y)=fX(x)
g'(x)
P(y⩽Y⩽y+Δy)=P(x⩽X⩽x+Δx)fy(y)·Δy=fX(x)·Δxfy(y)·g'(x)·Δx=fX(x)·Δxfy(y)=fX(x)g'(x) Part 2Let XX be a continuous random variable with fXfX as its PDF. The random variable YY is given as followsY=g(X)Y=g(X) where, gg is a differentiable, invertible & strictly decreasing function. That is,X=g-1(Y)X=g-1(Y)and for all x1x1 and x2x2 in the domain of gg, if x1<x2x1<x2g(x1)>g(x2)g(x1)>g(x2) Also, for all xx in the domain of gg, g'(x)<0g'(x)<0 GOAL : To find fYfY, which is the PDF of YY. For a sufficiently small Δx>0Δx>0,P(x⩽X⩽x+Δx)≈fX(x)·ΔxP(x⩽X⩽x+Δx)≈fX(x)·Δx y=g(x)y+Δy=g(x+Δx)y=g(x)y+Δy=g(x+Δx) Here, ΔyΔy is need not be equal to ΔxΔx. The quantity ΔyΔy denotes the change in yy corresponding to change in xx. Consider the derivative of g(x)g(x)g'(x)=limΔx→0g(x+Δx)-g(x)
Δxg'(x)=limΔx→0g(x+Δx)-g(x)Δx Since g(x+Δx)=y+Δyg(x+Δx)=y+Δy and g(x)=yg(x)=y, we getg'(x)=limΔx→0Δy
Δxg'(x)=limΔx→0ΔyΔx As Δx→0Δx→0Δy
Δx→g'(x)ΔyΔx→g'(x) Therefore, for sufficiently small ΔxΔx,Δy≈g'(x)·ΔxΔy≈g'(x)·Δx As Δx→0Δx→0• x+Δx→xx+Δx→x• g(x+Δx)→g(x)g(x+Δx)→g(x)• y+Δy→yy+Δy→y • Δy=(y+Δy)-y→0Δy=(y+Δy)-y→0 Since gg is an decreasing function, g(x)>g(x+Δx)g(x)>g(x+Δx). Therefore, Δy=g(x+Δx)-g(x)Δy=g(x+Δx)-g(x) is negative. The derivative of g(x)g(x) is also negative. Therefore, for sufficiently small ΔxΔx,|Δy|≈|g'(x)|·Δx|Δy|≈|g'(x)|·Δx Probability is between 00 and 11. Therefore,P(y+Δy⩽Y⩽y)≈fY(y)·|Δy|P(y+Δy⩽Y⩽y)≈fY(y)·|Δy| ClaimP(y+Δy⩽Y⩽y)=P(x⩽X⩽x+Δx)P(y+Δy⩽Y⩽y)=P(x⩽X⩽x+Δx) Proof :Consider the eventy+Δy⩽Y⩽yy+Δy⩽Y⩽y Substitute Y=g(X)Y=g(X)y+Δy⩽g(x)⩽yy+Δy⩽g(x)⩽y Since y=g(x)y=g(x) and y+Δy=g(x+Δx)y+Δy=g(x+Δx), we getg(x+Δx)⩽g(X)⩽g(x)g(x+Δx)⩽g(X)⩽g(x) Since gg is invertible and stricly decreasingg(x+Δx)⩽g(X)⩽g(x)g(x+Δx)⩽g(X)⩽g(x)impliesx⩽X⩽x+Δxx⩽X⩽x+Δx For all x∈[x,x+Δx]x^∈[x,x+Δx], g(x)∈ag(x+Δx),g(x)ag(x^)∈[g(x+Δx),g(x)]. Therefore,P(y+Δy⩽Y⩽y)=P(x⩽X⩽x+Δx)P(y+Δy⩽Y⩽y)=P(x⩽X⩽x+Δx) Recall |Δy|≈|g'(x)|·Δx|Δy|≈|g'(x)|·Δx.
P(y+Δy⩽Y⩽y)=P(x⩽X⩽x+Δx)
fy(y)·||Δy||=fX(x)·Δx
fy(y)·||g'(x)||·Δx=fX(x)·Δx
fy(y)=fX(x)
||g'(x)||
P(y+Δy⩽Y⩽y)=P(x⩽X⩽x+Δx)fy(y)·|Δy|=fX(x)·Δxfy(y)·|g'(x)|·Δx=fX(x)·Δxfy(y)=fX(x)|g'(x)|Part 3Part 1 result
fy(y)=fX(x)
g'(x)
fy(y)=fX(x)g'(x) Since g'(x)>0g'(x)>0, we have g'(x)=|g'(x)|g'(x)=|g'(x)|. Therefore,
fy(y)=fX(x)
||g'(x)||
fy(y)=fX(x)|g'(x)| Part 2 result
fy(y)=fX(x)
||g'(x)||
fy(y)=fX(x)|g'(x)| Combining both,
fy(y)=fX(x)
||g'(x)||
fy(y)=fX(x)|g'(x)| Since x=g-1(y)x=g-1(y)
fy(y)=fX(g-1(y))
|g'(g-1(y))|
fy(y)=fX(g-1(y))|g'(g-1(y))| The result holds when gg is differentiable and strictly monotonic (hence invertible), with g'(x)≠0g'(x)≠0
8. Transformation of Random Variables (two variable)Consider the following probability space⟨S,F,P⟩⟨S,F,P⟩where,• SS - Sample Space• FF - Event Space• PP - Probability Function XX and YY are two continuous random variables defined on the above probability space with fXYfXY as their joint PDF. The random variables PP and QQ are defined as follows
U=g(X,Y)
V=h(X,Y)
U=g(X,Y)V=h(X,Y)Also, there exist two functions mm and nn such thata
X=m(U,V)
Y=n(U,V)
X=m(U,V)Y=n(U,V) We have a one-to-one mapping between (X,Y)(X,Y) and (U,V)(U,V) Goal : To find the joint distribution fUVfUV Let ΔxΔx and ΔyΔy be real numbers. Let (x,y)(x,y) be in the domain of fXYfXY. The region R=[x,x+Δx]×[y,y+Δy] R=[x,x+Δx]×[y,y+Δy]forms a rectangle. For sufficiently small Δx>0Δx>0 and Δy>0Δy>0, the probability of (X,Y)(X,Y) being inside the region RR is the volume of the thin cuboid, with length as ΔxΔx, width as ΔyΔy and height as fXY(x,y)fXY(x,y) is P((X,Y)∈R)=P(x⩽X⩽x+Δx,y⩽Y⩽y+Δy)≈fXY(x,y)·Δx·ΔyP((X,Y)∈R)=P(x⩽X⩽x+Δx,y⩽Y⩽y+Δy)≈fXY(x,y)·Δx·Δy The vertices of the rectangular region RR are(x,y), (x+Δx,y), (x,y+Δy), (x+Δx,y+Δy)(x,y),(x+Δx,y),(x,y+Δy),(x+Δx,y+Δy) Let us find the mapping of these points, using the functions gg and hh ⟹(x,y)⟹(x,y)
g(x,y)=u
h(x,y)=v
g(x,y)=uh(x,y)=v(x,y)(x,y) maps to some (u,v)(u,v) in the domain of fUVfUV ⟹(x+Δx,y)⟹(x+Δx,y) By first order Taylor series approximation,
g(x+Δx,y)≈g(x,y)+(𝛿g

𝛿x(x,y)·Δx+𝛿g

𝛿y(x,y)·Δy)(u+𝛿g𝛿x(x,y)·Δx+𝛿g𝛿y(x,y)·Δy,v+𝛿h𝛿x(x,y)·Δx+𝛿h𝛿y(x,y)·Δy)• which is a+b+ca+b+c Clearly, the quadrilateral connecting aa, a+ba+b, a+ca+c and a+b+ca+b+c is a parallelogram. Let us call this region is ZZ. For sufficiently small ΔxΔx and ΔyΔy the following are be close to 00
𝛿g
𝛿x(x,y)·Δx		𝛿g
𝛿y(x,y)·Δy
𝛿h
𝛿x(x,y)·Δx		𝛿h
𝛿y(x,y)·Δy
𝛿g𝛿x(x,y)·Δx𝛿g𝛿y(x,y)·Δy𝛿h𝛿x(x,y)·Δx𝛿h𝛿y(x,y)·Δy As a result, for sufficiently small ΔxΔx and ΔyΔy, the vectors bb and cc are close to (0,0)(0,0). ThereforeP((U,V)∈Z)≈fUV(u,v)·Area(Z)P((U,V)∈Z)≈fUV(u,v)·Area(Z) Claim :P((U,V)∈Z)=P((X,Y)∈R)P((U,V)∈Z)=P((X,Y)∈R) Proof : We are going to prove this by showing that any point in the region RR is always mapped to a point in region ZZ and vice-versa. Let 𝛽∈[0,Δx]𝛽∈[0,Δx] and 𝜆∈[0,Δy]𝜆∈[0,Δy]. The point (x+𝛽,y+𝜆)(x+𝛽,y+𝜆) in in region RR. Let us find the mapping of (x+𝛽,y+𝜆)(x+𝛽,y+𝜆) using gg and hh. By first order Taylor series approximation
g(x+𝛽,y+𝜆)≈g(x,y)+(𝛿g

Δy∈[0,1]𝜆Δy∈[0,1] Therefore, the point (u,v)+(𝛿g
𝛿x(x,y)·𝛽,𝛿h

𝛿y(x,y)·𝜆)(u,v)+(𝛿g𝛿x(x,y)·𝛽,𝛿h𝛿x(x,y)·𝛽)+(𝛿g𝛿y(x,y)·𝜆,𝛿h𝛿y(x,y)·𝜆) lies inside the region ZZ. Since we have a one-to-one mapping between (X,Y)(X,Y) and (U,V)(U,V), using the function mm and nn, the point (u,v)+(𝛿g
𝛿x(x,y)·𝛽,𝛿h
𝛿x(x,y)·𝛽)+(𝛿g
𝛿y(x,y)·𝜆,𝛿h
𝛿y(x,y)·𝜆)(u,v)+(𝛿g𝛿x(x,y)·𝛽,𝛿h𝛿x(x,y)·𝛽)+(𝛿g𝛿y(x,y)·𝜆,𝛿h𝛿y(x,y)·𝜆) maps to (x+𝛽,y+𝜆)(x+𝛽,y+𝜆). Every point in region RR maps to a point in region ZZ and vice-versa. The set of all the outcomes in the sample space SS that are mapped to the region RR is given as follows.A={𝜔∈S|(X(𝜔),Y(𝜔))∈R}A={𝜔∈S|(X(𝜔),Y(𝜔))∈R} The set of all the outcomes in the sample space SS that are mapped to the region ZZ is given as follows.B={𝜔∈S|(U(𝜔),V(𝜔))∈Z}B={𝜔∈S|(U(𝜔),V(𝜔))∈Z} Every point in RR maps to ZZ and every point in ZZ maps to RR, the above sets are equal.• If 𝜔∈A𝜔∈A, then 𝜔∈B𝜔∈B• If 𝜔∈B𝜔∈B, then 𝜔∈A𝜔∈A Therefore,{𝜔|(X(𝜔),Y(𝜔))∈R}={𝜔|(U(𝜔),V(𝜔))∈Z}{𝜔|(X(𝜔),Y(𝜔))∈R}={𝜔|(U(𝜔),V(𝜔))∈Z} The above sets are subsets of sample space, and are therefore, the elements of FF. Every element of FF is an event. Hence,P((U,V)∈Z)=P((X,Y)∈R)P((U,V)∈Z)=P((X,Y)∈R) Let us substitute the following in the above equality.• P((X<Y)∈R)≈fXY(x,y)·Δx·ΔyP((X<Y)∈R)≈fXY(x,y)·Δx·Δy• P((U,V)∈Z)≈fUV(u,v)·Area(Z)P((U,V)∈Z)≈fUV(u,v)·Area(Z)
P((U,V)∈Z)=P((X<Y)∈R)
fUV(u,v)·Area(Z)=fXY(x,y)·Δx·Δy
fUV(u,v)=fXY(x,y)·Δx·Δy
Area(Z)
P((U,V)∈Z)=P((X<Y)∈R)fUV(u,v)·Area(Z)=fXY(x,y)·Δx·ΔyfUV(u,v)=fXY(x,y)·Δx·ΔyArea(Z) We have to find the area of the region ZZ. The vertices of the parallelogram (Z)(Z) are,a, a+b, a+c, a+b+c a,a+b,a+c,a+b+c The area of the above parallelogram is equal to the area of the parallelogram with the following vertices(0,0), b, c, b+c(0,0),b,c,b+c We have subtracted aa from every vertex. Subtracting aa from every vertex, moves the parallelogram along the R2R2 plane. It does not change the shape of the parallelogram.
bbccb+cb+c(0,0)(0,0)We have seen that the area of the above parallelogram is given as the absolute value of the determinant of the following matrix.a
||
bc
||
=a
𝛿g
𝛿x(x,y)·Δx		𝛿g
𝛿y(x,y)·Δy
𝛿h
𝛿x(x,y)·Δx		𝛿h
𝛿y(x,y)·Δy

)·Δx·Δydet([𝛿g𝛿x(x,y)·Δx𝛿g𝛿y(x,y)·Δy𝛿h𝛿x(x,y)·Δx𝛿h𝛿y(x,y)·Δy])=det([𝛿g𝛿x(x,y)𝛿g𝛿y(x,y)𝛿h𝛿x(x,y)𝛿h𝛿y(x,y)])·Δx·Δy The following is the Jacobian matrix.J=a

9. Bivariate Gaussian DistributionSuppose, X ~ N(0,1)X~N(0,1) and Y ~ N(0,1)Y~N(0,1) are two independent random variables. The random variables UU and VV are defined as followsa
U=g(X,Y)=𝛼1X+𝛼1Y
V=h(X,Y)=𝛽1X+𝛽2Y
U=g(X,Y)=𝛼1X+𝛼1YV=h(X,Y)=𝛽1X+𝛽2Y UU and VV can also be expressed as follows.a

fUV(u,v)=12𝜋e-((uv)(ATA)-1(uv))2|det(J)|=12𝜋·|det(J)|e-(uv)(AAT)-1(uv)2 Let us find the expected value and variance of UU and VV
E[U]=E[𝛼1X+𝛼2Y]
=𝛼1E[X]+𝛼2E[Y]
=0
E[U]=E[𝛼1X+𝛼2Y]=𝛼1E[X]+𝛼2E[Y]=0
E[V]=E[𝛽1X+𝛽2Y]
=𝛽1E[X]+𝛽2E[Y]
=0
E[V]=E[𝛽1X+𝛽2Y]=𝛽1E[X]+𝛽2E[Y]=0
Var(U)=E[U2]-(E[U])2
=E[𝛼21X2+2𝛼1𝛼2XY+𝛼22Y2]
=𝛼21E[X2]+𝛼22E[Y2]
=𝛼21+𝛼22
Var(U)=E[U2]-(E[U])2=E[𝛼12X2+2𝛼1𝛼2XY+𝛼22Y2]=𝛼12E[X2]+𝛼22E[Y2]=𝛼12+𝛼22Similarly, Var[V]=𝛽21+𝛽22Var[V]=𝛽12+𝛽22 • Since XX and YY are independent, E[XY]=E[X]·E[Y]=0E[XY]=E[X]·E[Y]=0• E[X2]=Var(X)+(E[X])2=1E[X2]=Var(X)+(E[X])2=1• Similarly, E[Y2]=1E[Y2]=1 Let us find the covariance of UU and VV
Cov(U,V)=Cov(𝛼1X+𝛼2Y,𝛽1X+𝛽2Y)
=𝛼1𝛽1Cov(X,X)+𝛼1𝛽2Cov(X,Y)+𝛼2𝛽1Cov(X,Y)+𝛼2𝛽2Cov(Y,Y)
=𝛼1𝛽1Var(X)+𝛼2𝛽2Var(Y)
=𝛼1𝛽1+𝛼2𝛽2
Cov(U,V)=Cov(𝛼1X+𝛼2Y,𝛽1X+𝛽2Y)=𝛼1𝛽1Cov(X,X)+𝛼1𝛽2Cov(X,Y)+𝛼2𝛽1Cov(X,Y)+𝛼2𝛽2Cov(Y,Y)=𝛼1𝛽1Var(X)+𝛼2𝛽2Var(Y)=𝛼1𝛽1+𝛼2𝛽2 We have used the followins facts• Cov(X,X)=Var(X)Cov(X,X)=Var(X)• Cov(P,aQ+bR)=aCov(P,Q)+bCov(P,R)Cov(P,aQ+bR)=aCov(P,Q)+bCov(P,R)• Cov(X,Y)=0Cov(X,Y)=0 as XX and YY are independent. Let us find AATAAT
AAT=(a

=a
𝛼21+𝛼22𝛼1𝛽1+𝛼2𝛽2
𝛼1𝛽1+𝛼2𝛽2𝛽21+𝛽22
=a
Var(U)Cov(U,V)
Cov(U,V)Var(V)
AAT=([𝛼1𝛼2𝛽1𝛽2]×[𝛼1𝛽1𝛼2𝛽2])=[𝛼12+𝛼22𝛼1𝛽1+𝛼2𝛽2𝛼1𝛽1+𝛼2𝛽2𝛽12+𝛽22]=[Var(U)Cov(U,V)Cov(U,V)Var(V)] AATAAT is the covariance matrix. Let us find the determinant of the Jacobian matrix
det(a
𝛼1𝛼2
𝛽1𝛽2
)		=𝛼1𝛽2-𝛼2𝛽1
det([𝛼1𝛼2𝛽1𝛽2])=𝛼1𝛽2-𝛼2𝛽1 Note that the Jacobian matrix is the same as matrix AA. Therefore,det(J)=det(A)=(det(A))2=det(A)×det(AT)=det(AAT)det(J)=det(A)=(det(A))2=det(A)×det(AT)=det(AAT) The determinant of the Jacobian matrix is the square root of the determinant of the covariance matrix.
10. Multivariate Gaussian DistributionConsider the following random vector.X=a
X1
X2
⋮
Xn
X=[X1X2⋮Xn] X ~ N(𝜇,𝛴)X~N(𝜇,𝛴), where 𝜇∈Rn𝜇∈Rn and 𝛴𝛴 is a n×nn×n covariance matrix.𝛴=a
Var(X1)Cov(X1,X2)⋯Cov(X1,Xn)
Cov(X2,X1)Var(X2)⋯Cov(X2,Xn)
⋮⋮⋱⋮
Cov(Xn,X1)Cov(Xn,X2)⋯Var(Xn)
𝛴=[Var(X1)Cov(X1,X2)⋯Cov(X1,Xn)Cov(X2,X1)Var(X2)⋯Cov(X2,Xn)⋮⋮⋱⋮Cov(Xn,X1)Cov(Xn,X2)⋯Var(Xn)] The PDF of XX, given by fX(x)fX(x) is the joint distribution of X1,...,XnX1,...,Xn, which is given as followsfX1,...,Xn(x1,...,xn)=fX(x)=1
(2𝜋)n/2|det(𝛴)|e-(x-𝜇)T𝛴-1(x-𝜇)
2fX1,...,Xn(x1,...,xn)=fX(x)=1(2𝜋)n/2|det(𝛴)|e-(x-𝜇)T𝛴-1(x-𝜇)2 Here, x=[x1,...,xn]Tx=[x1,...,xn]T and (x-𝜇)(x-𝜇) is a column vector Compare this to the PDF of univariate Gaussian.1
2𝜋𝜎e-(x-𝜇)2
2𝜎212𝜋𝜎e-(x-𝜇)22𝜎2 • 𝜎2𝜎2 is in the denominator of -(x-𝜇)2
2𝜎2-(x-𝜇)22𝜎2. Therefore, (𝜎2)-1(𝜎2)-1 will be in the numerator. Compare this to the 𝛴-1𝛴-1 in the multivariate Gaussian distribution• In univariate Gaussian, we have (x-𝜇)2(x-𝜇)2. In multivariate Gaussian, we have (x-𝜇)T𝛴-1(x-𝜇)(x-𝜇)T𝛴-1(x-𝜇)• In univariate Gaussian, we have 𝜎𝜎 in the denominator of the PDF. Compare this to |det(𝛴)||det(𝛴)| in the denominator of the PDF of the multivariate Gaussian.
11. Bivariate Gaussian (Plot)
 Readers are requested to have a look at the above plot and see the impact of variance and covariance on the generated samples. To understand the variance, have a look at the range of XX axis and YY axis. To understand the covariance, kindly have a look at the orientation of the scatter plot.
12. Maximum LikelihoodWe shall understand maximum likelihood with examples. Example 1Given X1,X2
and
 X3
~
iid
Bernoulli
X1,X2andX3~iidBernoulli\math-container. When a coin is tossed, for i∈{1,2,3}i∈{1,2,3}, XiXi shows heads with probability pp and tails with probability (1-p)(1-p). Here, pp is unknown. Let us denote heads with 11 and tails with 00. Suppose we toss the coin thrice and see heads on first toss, tails on second toss, and heads on third toss. Let us denote the outcomes of the three tosses with small 'xx': x1=1x1=1, x2=0x2=0 and x3=1x3=1. Here, capital 'XX' is used for the random variable and small 'xx' is used for the samples that have been observed. Then likelihood function, which takes the unknown parameter pp and the observed sampes as input, is given byL(p;{x1,x2,x3})=P(X1=x1,X2=x2,X3=x3|p)L(p;{x1,x2,x3})=P(X1=x1,X2=x2,X3=x3|p) The likelihood function is a joint distribution of the random variables. Since X1,X2X1,X2 and X3X3 are i.i.d random variables, we can write the likelihood function as follows: L(p;{x1,x2,x3})=P(X1=x1|p)×P(X2=x2|p)×P(X3=x3|p)L(p;{x1,x2,x3})=P(X1=x1|p)×P(X2=x2|p)×P(X3=x3|p) P(Xi=1|p)=pP(Xi=1|p)=p and P(Xi=0|p)=(1-p)P(Xi=0|p)=(1-p). We can therefore, write the likelihood function as: L(p;{x1,x2,x3})=p×(1-p)×p=p2(1-p)L(p;{x1,x2,x3})=p×(1-p)×p=p2(1-p) Let us plot the likelihood function.
ppLL2/32/3pp is unknown. But, it can take any value from 00 to 11. For different values of pp, the likelihood function gives the joint probability of observing x1=1x1=1, x2=0x2=0 and x3=1x3=1. Since pp is unknown, we have to estimate pp. One way to estimate pp is to find the value of pp that maximizes the likelihood function. That is,p=
argmax
paL(p;{x1,x2,x3})ap=argmaxp[L(p;{x1,x2,x3})]For the likelihood function plotted above, we can see that it is maximum when p=2
3p=23. In our example, 2
323 is the ratio of the total number of heads and the total number of outcomes. If the given random variables are assumed to be discrete, then we maximize the joint probability mass function. If the given random variables are assumed to be continuous, we maximize the joint probability density function. Example 2X1,...,XnX1,...,Xn are nn i.i.d random variables from continuous Uniform(a,b)Uniform(a,b). Suppose we have nn samples x1,...,xnx1,...,xn, where xixi is a sample of the random variable XiXi, for i∈{1,...,n}i∈{1,...,n} Using the samples, we have to estimate aa and bb. Following is the likelihood function.
L(a,b,{x1,...,xn})=fX1,...,Xn|a,b(x1,...,xn)
L(a,b,{x1,...,xn})=fX1,...,Xn|a,b(x1,...,xn)Consider the following indicator function.
1(xi)=aa
1 if xi∈[a,b], ∀i∈{1,...,n}
0 if xi∉[a,b], ∀i∈{1,...,n}
1(xi)={1ifxi∈[a,b],∀i∈{1,...,n}0ifxi∉[a,b],∀i∈{1,...,n} Since X1,...,XnX1,...,Xn are i.i.d,
L(a,b,{x1,...,xn})=n∏i=1fXi|a,b(xi)
=n∏i=11
b-a·1(xi)
=(b-a)-n·n∏i=11(xi)
L(a,b,{x1,...,xn})=∏i=1nfXi|a,b(xi)=∏i=1n1b-a·1(xi)=(b-a)-n·∏i=1n1(xi) We have to maximize the likelihood function. Since loglog is an increasing function, we can maximize the log likelihood function. We shall use (log i.e., loge)(logi.e.,loge)
logL(a,b,{x1,...,xn})=log((b-a)-n·n∏i=11(xi))
=-nlog(b-a)+n∑i=1log(1(xi))
logL(a,b,{x1,...,xn})=log((b-a)-n·∏i=1n1(xi))=-nlog(b-a)+∑i=1nlog(1(xi))If one of the samples xixi is not in [a,b][a,b], then 1(xi)=01(xi)=0. Since log(x)→-∞log(x)→-∞ as x→0x→0, the entire log likelihood function approaches -∞-∞. Without loss of generality, we shall assume that b⩾max(x1,...,xn)b⩾max(x1,...,xn) and a⩽min(x1,...,xn)a⩽min(x1,...,xn). In that case, for all i∈{1,...,n}i∈{1,...,n}, 1(xi)=11(xi)=1, and therefore, log(1(xi))=0log(1(xi))=0. Goal
max
a,b[-nlog(b-a)]maxa,b[-nlog(b-a)] Equivalently, we can minimize the following.
min
a,b[nlog(b-a)]mina,b[nlog(b-a)] The objective function will be minimum when b-ab-a is minimum. However, we have the following constraints.a⩽min(x1,...,xn)b⩾max(x1,...,xn)a⩽min(x1,...,xn)b⩾max(x1,...,xn) b-ab-a is minimum whena=min(x1,...,xn)b=max(x1,...,xn)a=min(x1,...,xn)b=max(x1,...,xn) Example 3X1,...,XnX1,...,Xn are nn i.i.d random variables from Normal(𝜇,I)Normal(𝜇,I), where 𝜇∈Rd𝜇∈Rd and II is the d×dd×d identity matrix. Suppose we have nn samples x1,...,xnx1,...,xn, where xixi is a sample of the random variable XiXi, for i∈{1,...,n}i∈{1,...,n} Using the samples, we have to estimate 𝜇𝜇. Following is the likelihood function.
L(𝜇,{x1,...,xn})=fX1,...,Xn|𝜇(x1,...,xn)
L(𝜇,{x1,...,xn})=fX1,...,Xn|𝜇(x1,...,xn) Since X1,...,XnX1,...,Xn are i.i.d,
L(𝜇,{x1,...,xn})=n∏i=1fXi|𝜇(xi)
=n∏i=11
(2𝜋)d|det(I)|e-(xi-𝜇)TI-1(xi-𝜇)
2
=n∏i=11
(2𝜋)de-(xTixi-2𝜇Txi+𝜇T𝜇)
2
L(𝜇,{x1,...,xn})=∏i=1nfXi|𝜇(xi)=∏i=1n1(2𝜋)d|det(I)|e-(xi-𝜇)TI-1(xi-𝜇)2=∏i=1n1(2𝜋)de-(xiTxi-2𝜇Txi+𝜇T𝜇)2Note on simplification::
(xi-𝜇)T(xi-𝜇)=xTixi-xTi𝜇-𝜇Txi+𝜇T𝜇
=xTixi-2𝜇Txi+𝜇T𝜇
(xi-𝜇)T(xi-𝜇)=xiTxi-xiT𝜇-𝜇Txi+𝜇T𝜇=xiTxi-2𝜇Txi+𝜇T𝜇Note : xTi𝜇=𝜇TxixiT𝜇=𝜇Txi With loglog as logeloge, the log likelhood function is given as follows.
logL(𝜇,{x1,...,xn})=log(n∏i=11
(2𝜋)de-(xTixi-2𝜇Txi+𝜇T𝜇)
2)
=n∑i=1[log(1
(2𝜋)d)·(-1
2(xTixi-2𝜇Txi+𝜇T𝜇))]
=n
2log(1
(2𝜋)d)n∑i=1-(xTixi-2𝜇Txi+𝜇T𝜇)
logL(𝜇,{x1,...,xn})=log(∏i=1n1(2𝜋)de-(xiTxi-2𝜇Txi+𝜇T𝜇)2)=∑i=1n[log(1(2𝜋)d)·(-12(xiTxi-2𝜇Txi+𝜇T𝜇))]=n2log(1(2𝜋)d)∑i=1n-(xiTxi-2𝜇Txi+𝜇T𝜇) Ignoring the constants, we have to maximize the following
max
𝜇[n∑i=1-(xTixi-2𝜇Txi+𝜇T𝜇)]max𝜇[∑i=1n-(xiTxi-2𝜇Txi+𝜇T𝜇)]Instead, we can minimize the following
min
𝜇n∑i=1(xTixi-2𝜇Txi+𝜇T𝜇)min𝜇∑i=1n(xiTxi-2𝜇Txi+𝜇T𝜇) Let us simplify the objective function.a
n∑i=1(xTixi-2𝜇Txi+𝜇T𝜇)=(n∑i=1xTixi)-(n∑i=1𝜇Txi)+(n∑i=1𝜇T𝜇)
=(n∑i=1xTixi)-(𝜇Tn∑i=1xi)+(n·𝜇T𝜇)
∑i=1n(xiTxi-2𝜇Txi+𝜇T𝜇)=(∑i=1nxiTxi)-(∑i=1n𝜇Txi)+(∑i=1n𝜇T𝜇)=(∑i=1nxiTxi)-(𝜇T∑i=1nxi)+(n·𝜇T𝜇) Notice,• n·𝜇T𝜇n·𝜇T𝜇 is a convex (quadratic) function 𝜇𝜇• -(𝜇Tn∑i=1xi)-(𝜇T∑i=1nxi) is a convex (linear) function of 𝜇𝜇• (n∑i=1xTixi)(∑i=1nxiTxi) is a constant The objective function is therefore, convex. At the global minimum, the gradient will be zero. We can minimze the following function.
min
𝜇f(𝜇)min𝜇f(𝜇)where,f(𝜇)=n·𝜇T𝜇-(𝜇Tn∑i=1xi)f(𝜇)=n·𝜇T𝜇-(𝜇T∑i=1nxi) Let us find the gradient of 𝜇𝜇
∇f(𝜇)=n𝜇-n∑i=1xi
∇f(𝜇)=n𝜇-∑i=1nxi Equate ∇f(𝜇)∇f(𝜇) with 00 to find the optimal 𝜇𝜇
∇f(𝜇)=0
n𝜇-n∑i=1xi=0
𝜇=1
nn∑i=1xi
∇f(𝜇)=0n𝜇-∑i=1nxi=0𝜇=1n∑i=1nxi The optimal 𝜇𝜇 is the average of the observed samples.
13. Jensen's InequalityLet f:Rd→Rf:Rd→R be a concave function. Consider 𝜆1,...,𝜆n∈[0,1]𝜆1,...,𝜆n∈[0,1] and n∑i=1𝜆i=1∑i=1n𝜆i=1. For all x1,...,xnx1,...,xn in RdRd, the following inequality holds truef(𝜆1x1+....+𝜆nxn)⩾𝜆1f(x1)+...+𝜆nf(xn)f(𝜆1x1+....+𝜆nxn)⩾𝜆1f(x1)+...+𝜆nf(xn)
14. Gaussian Mixture ModelIntrodcutionSuppose, we have nn i.i.d samples x1,...,xnx1,...,xn that look as follows.
From which distribution are these data points from? The data points are clustered in three different regions. The data points could have been generate from a mixture of three gaussians. Consider the following.• There are three mixtures.– Mixture 1 : N(𝜇1,𝛴1)N(𝜇1,𝛴1)– Mixture 2 : N(𝜇2,𝛴2)N(𝜇2,𝛴2)– Mixture 3 : N(𝜇3,𝛴3)N(𝜇3,𝛴3)• Each mixture has a probability 𝜋i∈[0,1], i∈{1,2,3}𝜋i∈[0,1],i∈{1,2,3} such that 𝜋1+𝜋2+𝜋3=1𝜋1+𝜋2+𝜋3=1 GENERATIVE STORY : Each sample is generated as follows• Choose a mixture Z∈{1,2,3}Z∈{1,2,3} with probability 𝜋i𝜋i.P(Z=i)=𝜋i,
where
 i∈{1,2,3}P(Z=i)=𝜋i,wherei∈{1,2,3}• From the choosen mixture ii, generate a sample xx from the following distributionN(𝜇i,𝛴i)N(𝜇i,𝛴i) Given the samples, our goal is to estimate the following𝜋1,𝜋2,𝜋3, 𝜇1,𝜇2,𝜇3, 𝛴1,𝛴2,𝛴3𝜋1,𝜋2,𝜋3,𝜇1,𝜇2,𝜇3,𝛴1,𝛴2,𝛴3 Likelihood FunctionWe have nn i.i.d samples D={x1,...,xn}D={x1,...,xn} in RdRd, generated from a mixture of kk Gaussian distributions. In general, we might not know kk beforehand. Therefore, we experiment with different values for kk. Our goal is to estimate the following. For i∈{1,...,k}i∈{1,...,k}• Mixture probabilities. Here, 𝜋i∈[0,1],
where
 i∈{1,...,k} 𝜋i∈[0,1],wherei∈{1,...,k} and 𝜋1+...+𝜋k=1𝜋1+...+𝜋k=1𝜋1,..,𝜋k𝜋1,..,𝜋k• Mean of each mixture𝜇1,...,𝜇k∈Rd𝜇1,...,𝜇k∈Rd• Covariance matrix of each mixture𝛴1,...,𝛴k∈Rd×d𝛴1,...,𝛴k∈Rd×d Here is the likelihood function. We shall comprehensively denote all the parameters as 𝜃𝜃
L(𝜃,D)=n∏i=1k∑j=1𝜋kj·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)
L(𝜃,D)=∏i=1n∑j=1k𝜋kj·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)Here, N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j) implies1
(2𝜋)d/2|det(𝛴j)|e-1
2(xi-𝜇j)T𝛴-1j(xi-𝜇j)1(2𝜋)d/2|det(𝛴j)|e-12(xi-𝜇j)T𝛴j-1(xi-𝜇j) Let us unpack the likelihood function.k∑j=1𝜋j·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)∑j=1k𝜋j·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)This can be written as follows:𝜋1·N(xi;𝜇1,𝛴1)+...+𝜋j·N(xi;𝜇k,𝛴k)𝜋1·N(xi;𝜇1,𝛴1)+...+𝜋j·N(xi;𝜇k,𝛴k) Each term𝜋j·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)𝜋j·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j) represents the contribution to the density of the sample xixi from cluster jj. Here:• 𝜋j𝜋j denotes the probability of choosing cluster jj• N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j) denotes the density of xixi​ under cluster jj The product 𝜋j·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)𝜋j·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j) captures the joint contribution of:• selecting cluster jj, and• generating the sample xixi​ from that cluster The summation k∑j=1∑j=1k ink∑j=1𝜋j·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)∑j=1k𝜋j·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)captures the fact that the sample xixi​ could have been generated from any one of the kk clusters. Since the cluster identity is unknown, we sum over all possible clusters. For a dataset of nn independent samples, the likelihood is:n∏i=1k∑j=1𝜋kj·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)∏i=1n∑j=1k𝜋kj·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)The product n∏i=1 ∏i=1ncaptures the joint density of all samples, using the assumption that the samples are independent. With loglog as logeloge, the log likelihood is given as followsa
logL(𝜃,D)=log(n∏i=1k∑j=1𝜋kj·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j))
=n∑i=1log(k∑j=1𝜋kj·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j))
logL(𝜃,D)=log(∏i=1n∑j=1k𝜋kj·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j))=∑i=1nlog(∑j=1k𝜋kj·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j))Jensen's InequalityIn the log likelihood function, we have summation inside loglog. In general, it is difficult to deal with summation inside loglog. For each i∈{1,...,n}i∈{1,...,n} and for each j∈{1,...,k}j∈{1,...,k}, let us introduce a new variable z(i,j)∈[0,1]z(i,j)∈[0,1] such that for each i∈{1,...,n}i∈{1,...,n}z(i,1)+...+z(i,k)=1z(i,1)+...+z(i,k)=1 We shall multiply and divide with z(i,j)z(i,j)
n∑i=1log(k∑j=1𝜋kj·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j))=n∑i=1log(k∑j=1(z(i,j))𝜋kj·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)
z(i,j))
∑i=1nlog(∑j=1k𝜋kj·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j))=∑i=1nlog(∑j=1k(z(i,j))𝜋kj·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)z(i,j)) loglog is a concave function. Using Jensen's inequality,n∑i=1log(k∑j=1(z(i,j))𝜋kj·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)
z(i,j))⩾n∑i=1k∑j=1(z(i,j))·log(𝜋kj·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)
z(i,j))∑i=1nlog(∑j=1k(z(i,j))𝜋kj·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)z(i,j))⩾∑i=1n∑j=1k(z(i,j))·log(𝜋kj·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)z(i,j)) We shall later see why this is useful. For now, let us focus on the modified log likelihood, which is denoted as M(𝜃)M(𝜃)M(𝜃)=n∑i=1k∑j=1(z(i,j))·log(𝜋kj·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)
z(i,j))M(𝜃)=∑i=1n∑j=1k(z(i,j))·log(𝜋kj·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)z(i,j)) Maximizing the Modified Log LikelihoodWhile we shall not derive the maximum of the modified log likelihood function with respect to z(i,j), 𝜋j, 𝜇jz(i,j),𝜋j,𝜇j and 𝛴j𝛴j, we shall, however, list the results and interpret the expression. Fixing 𝜋j, 𝜇j𝜋j,𝜇j and 𝛴j𝛴j as constants, the maximizing value of z(i,j)z(i,j) for the modified log likelihood is given as follows.z(i,j)=P(Z=j|X=xi)=𝜋j·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)
k∑q=1𝜋q·N(xi;𝜇q,𝛴q)=P(Z=j)·fX|Z=j(xi)
k∑q=1P(Z=q)·fX|Z=q(xi)z(i,j)=P(Z=j|X=xi)=𝜋j·N(xi;𝜇j,𝛴j)∑q=1k𝜋q·N(xi;𝜇q,𝛴q)=P(Z=j)·fX|Z=j(xi)∑q=1kP(Z=q)·fX|Z=q(xi) Interpretation of z(i,j)z(i,j) : Given that the data point xixi is observed, z(i,j)z(i,j) denotes the probability that it belong to cluster jj. Fixing z(i,j)z(i,j) as constants, the maximizing values of 𝜋j,𝜇j𝜋j,𝜇j and 𝛴j𝛴j for the modified log likelihood is given as follows. Let Nj=n∑i=1z(i,j)Nj=∑i=1nz(i,j). Recall that nn is the number of samples.
𝜋j=Nj
n		𝜇j=1
Njn∑i=1z(i,j)·xi		𝛴j=1
Njn∑i=1z(i,j)(xi-𝜇j)(xi-𝜇j)T
𝜋j=Njn𝜇j=1Nj∑i=1nz(i,j)·xi𝛴j=1Nj∑i=1nz(i,j)(xi-𝜇j)(xi-𝜇j)T For each data point xixi and for each cluster jj, z(i,j)z(i,j) denotes the probability that xixi belongs to the jthjth cluster. For the jthjth cluster, NjNj denotes the sum of z(i,j)z(i,j) for all i∈{1,...,n}i∈{1,...,n} EM AlgorithmHere is the algorithm to maximize the original log likelihood function. Step - 1 - Arbitrarily initialize 𝜋(0)j,𝜇(0)j𝜋j(0),𝜇j(0) and 𝛴(0)j𝛴j(0), for all j∈{1,...,k}j∈{1,...,k}. Here 00 denotes the time step tt Step - 2 - For t∈{0,1,2,....,T}t∈{0,1,2,....,T}, repeat the following steps • (E - Step) - This is called the expectation step. Using 𝜋(t)j,𝜇(t)j
and
 𝛴(t)j𝜋j(t),𝜇j(t)and𝛴j(t) compute z(t+1)(i,j)z(i,j)(t+1) for all i∈{1,...,n}i∈{1,...,n} and for all j∈{1,...,k}j∈{1,...,k}z(t+1)(i,j)=𝜋(t)j·N(xi;𝜇(t)j,𝛴(t)j)
k∑q=1𝜋(t)q·N(xi;𝜇(t)q,𝛴(t)q)z(i,j)(t+1)=𝜋j(t)·N(xi;𝜇j(t),𝛴j(t))∑q=1k𝜋q(t)·N(xi;𝜇q(t),𝛴q(t))• (M - Step) - This is called the maximization step. Using zt+1(i,j)z(i,j)t+1, compute 𝜋tj,𝜇tj
and
 𝛴tj𝜋jt,𝜇jtand𝛴jt for all j∈{1,...,k}j∈{1,...,k}
𝜋(t+1)j=N(t+1)j
n		𝜇(t+1)j=1
N(t+1)jn∑i=1z(t+1)(i,j)·xi		𝛴(t+1)j=1
N(t+1)jn∑i=1z(t+1)(i,j)(xi-𝜇j)(xi-𝜇j)T
𝜋j(t+1)=Nj(t+1)n𝜇j(t+1)=1Nj(t+1)∑i=1nz(i,j)(t+1)·xi𝛴j(t+1)=1Nj(t+1)∑i=1nz(i,j)(t+1)(xi-𝜇j)(xi-𝜇j)TEnd Geometric Perspective of EM Algorithm
𝜃t𝜃t𝜃t+1𝜃t+1𝜃t+2𝜃t+2 The above picture is just to illustrate how EM algorithm works. Assume that the red curve is the original log likelihood function. At 𝜃t𝜃t, we construct a lower bound, which is the modified log likelihood (green curve). We maximize the modified log likelihood and move to 𝜃t+1𝜃t+1. At 𝜃t+1𝜃t+1, we construct another lower bound, which, once again is the modified log likelihood (green curve). By maximizing the modified log likelihood, we move to 𝜃t+2𝜃t+2. In the given picture, 𝜃t+2𝜃t+2 is the local maximum of the log likelihood. EM algorithm is guaranteed to converge to a local maximum.
15. Concentration InequalitesMarkov's Inequality XX is a non-negative random variable, i.e., the support of XX is non-negative. Let a>0a>0. ThenP(X⩾a)⩽E[X]
aP(X⩾a)⩽E[X]aProof : We shall prove the inequality for XX being a continuous random variable with fXfX as PDF. If XX is discrete, a similar proof can be show.
E[X]=∞∫0fX(x)·x dx
=a∫0fX(x)·x dx+∞∫afX(x)·x dx
⩾∞∫afX(x)·x dx
E[X]=∫0∞fX(x)·xdx=∫0afX(x)·xdx+∫a∞fX(x)·xdx⩾∫a∞fX(x)·xdxThe above step is justified asa∫0fX(x)·x dx⩾0∫0afX(x)·xdx⩾0
a∫0fX(x)·x dx⩾∞∫afX(x)·a dx
⩾a∞∫afX(x) dx
⩾a·P(X⩾a)
∫0afX(x)·xdx⩾∫a∞fX(x)·adx⩾a∫a∞fX(x)dx⩾a·P(X⩾a)The above step is also justified as aa is the smallest value in the interval [a,∞)[a,∞). We have,E[X]⩾∞∫afX(x)·x dx⩾a·P(X⩾a)E[X]⩾∫a∞fX(x)·xdx⩾a·P(X⩾a)Therefore,
a·P(X⩾a)⩽E[X]
P(X⩾a)⩽E[X]
a
a·P(X⩾a)⩽E[X]P(X⩾a)⩽E[X]a Interpretation : The Markov's inequality says that the probability of XX taking a value greater than or equal to a>0a>0 is upper bounded by E[X]
aE[X]a. As aa increases, the upper bound decays linearly. Chebyshev's InequalityLet XX be a random variable with 𝜇𝜇 as its expected value and 𝜎2𝜎2 as its variance. Let t>0t>0. Then,P(|X-𝜇|⩾t)⩽𝜎2
t2P(|X-𝜇|⩾t)⩽𝜎2t2Proof :
P(|X-𝜇|⩾t)=P((X-𝜇)2⩾t2)
P(|X-𝜇|⩾t)=P((X-𝜇)2⩾t2)Since |X-𝜇|⩾0|X-𝜇|⩾0 and t>0t>0, squaring on both the sides doesn't change the inequality. (X-𝜇)2(X-𝜇)2 is a non-negative random variable. By Markov's inequality,
P((X-𝜇)2⩾t2)⩽E[(X-𝜇)2]
t2
⩽𝜎2
t2
P((X-𝜇)2⩾t2)⩽E[(X-𝜇)2]t2⩽𝜎2t2E[(X-𝜇)2]E[(X-𝜇)2] is the variance of XX. Since a
P(|X-𝜇|⩾t)=P((X-𝜇)2⩾t2)
P(|X-𝜇|⩾t)=P((X-𝜇)2⩾t2), we getP(|X-𝜇|⩾t)⩽𝜎2
t2P(|X-𝜇|⩾t)⩽𝜎2t2Interpretation : Let t=k𝜎t=k𝜎, where k>0k>0. Then,
P(|X-𝜇|⩾t)⩽𝜎2
t2
P(|X-𝜇|⩾k𝜎)⩽𝜎2
k2𝜎2
P(|X-𝜇|⩾k𝜎)⩽1
k2
P(|X-𝜇|⩾t)⩽𝜎2t2P(|X-𝜇|⩾k𝜎)⩽𝜎2k2𝜎2P(|X-𝜇|⩾k𝜎)⩽1k2The probability of |X-𝜇||X-𝜇| being greater than kk times the standard deviation 𝜎𝜎 is upper bounded by 1
k21k2. As kk increases, the upper bound decays quadratically. Heoffding's InequalityLet X1,...,XnX1,...,Xn be i.i.d random variables with expected value 𝜇𝜇 and Xi∈[a,b]Xi∈[a,b] for all i∈{1,...,n}i∈{1,...,n}. ⏨XX¯ is given as follows⏨X=1
n(X1+...+Xn)X¯=1n(X1+...+Xn) Let t>0t>0. Then,P(|⏨X-𝜇|⩾t)⩽2e-2nt2
(b-a)2P(|X¯-𝜇|⩾t)⩽2e-2nt2(b-a)2 We shall not prove this inequality. However, here is an interpretation of the upper bound. LetU=2e-2nt2
(b-a)2U=2e-2nt2(b-a)2 • As nn increases, UU decreases. The upper bound decays exponentially as nn increases• As tt increases, UU decreases. The upper bound decays exponentially as tt increases• As (b-a)2(b-a)2 increases, UU increases. Similarly, as (b-a)2(b-a)2 decreases, UU decreases. • As the difference between aa and bb grows, the upper bound increases. A larger interval leads to a higher upper bound for the absolute deviation of ⏨XX¯ from 𝜇𝜇• As the difference between aa and bb shrinks, the upper bound decreases. A smaller interval leads to a lower upper bound for the absolute deviation of ⏨XX¯ from 𝜇𝜇
16. Convergence in Probability and Convergence DistributionLike sequence of numbers, we can also have sequence of random variables. As an example, consider YY to be a random variable. X1,...,XnX1,...,Xn is a sequence of random variable, whereXi=Y
i,
where
 i∈{1,...,n}Xi=Yi,wherei∈{1,...,n}Convergence in ProbabilityLet ZZ be a random variable. A sequence of random variables X1,...,XnX1,...,Xn converges to ZZ in probability, shown by XnP⏪⏪⏫ ZXn→PZ, if for all 𝜖>0𝜖>0limn→∞P(|Xn-Z|⩾𝜖)=0limn→∞P(|Xn-Z|⩾𝜖)=0 Recall that a random variable is a function from the samlple space SS to RR. Each element in SS is an outcome. When we write P(|Xn-Z|⩾𝜖)P(|Xn-Z|⩾𝜖), we can interpret it as followsPa{𝜔∈S:|Xn(𝜔)-Z(𝜔)|⩾𝜖}aP({𝜔∈S:|Xn(𝜔)-Z(𝜔)|⩾𝜖}) Now,limn→∞Pa{𝜔∈S:|Xn(𝜔)-Z(𝜔)|⩾𝜖}a=0limn→∞P({𝜔∈S:|Xn(𝜔)-Z(𝜔)|⩾𝜖})=0 As nn increases, the probability of those outcomes 𝜔𝜔 for which Xn(𝜔)Xn(𝜔) and Z(𝜔)Z(𝜔) differ in absolute value by more than 𝜖𝜖 goes to 00. Convergence in DistributionLet ZZ be a random variable. A sequence of random variables X1,...,XnX1,...,Xn converges to ZZ in distribution, shown by XnD⏪⏪⏫ ZXn→DZ, iflimn→∞FXn(x)=FZ(x)limn→∞FXn(x)=FZ(x)for all xx at which FZFZ is continuous. As nn increase, P(Xn⩽x)P(Xn⩽x) approaches P(Z⩽x)P(Z⩽x). As nn increases, the shape of the distribution XnXn gradually matches the shape of the distribution ZZ. ComparisionConvergence in probability is stronger than convergence in distribution. That is, if XnP⏪⏪⏫ ZXn→PZ then XnD⏪⏪⏫ ZXn→DZ. However, the converse need not be true.
17. Weak Law of Large NumbersX1,...,XnX1,...,Xn are i.i.d random variables with E[Xi]=𝜇E[Xi]=𝜇 for all i∈{1,...,n}i∈{1,...,n}. ⏨XX¯ is given as follows⏨X=1
n(X1+...+Xn)X¯=1n(X1+...+Xn) The weak law of large numbers states that ⏨XX¯ converges to 𝜇𝜇 in probability. That is, ⏨XP⏪⏪⏫ 𝜇X¯→P𝜇In other words, for any 𝜖>0𝜖>0limn→∞P(|⏨X-𝜇|⩾𝜖)=0limn→∞P(|X¯-𝜇|⩾𝜖)=0 As the number of samples increases, the sample mean ⏨XX¯ becomes close to 𝜇𝜇 with high probability.
18. Central Limit TheoremX1,...,XnX1,...,Xn are i.i.d random variables with E[Xi]=𝜇E[Xi]=𝜇 and Var(Xi)=𝜎2Var(Xi)=𝜎2 for all i∈{1,...,n}i∈{1,...,n}. ⏨XX¯ is given as follows⏨X=1
n(X1+...+Xn)X¯=1n(X1+...+Xn) ZnZn is given as follows.Zn=⏨X-𝜇
𝜎/n=X1+...+Xn-n𝜇
𝜎nZn=X¯-𝜇𝜎/n=X1+...+Xn-n𝜇𝜎n Let A ~ Normal(0,1)A~Normal(0,1). The central limit theorem states that ZnZn converges to AA in distribution. That is,ZnD⏪⏪⏫ AZn→DAIn other words,limn→∞FZn(z)=FA(z)limn→∞FZn(z)=FA(z)where, FAFA is the CDF of Normal(0,1)Normal(0,1). Interpretation : As nn increases, ZnZn which is the standardized version of ⏨XX¯, becomes similar in shape to the standard normal distribution. As nn increases,• ⏨XX¯ becomes normal, centered at 𝜇𝜇, with variance 𝜎2
n𝜎2n• ZnZn becomes standard normal, centered at 00, with variance 11 Let us see why E[⏨X]=𝜇E[X¯]=𝜇 and why Var(⏨X)=𝜎2
nVar(X¯)=𝜎2n.
E[⏨X]=E[1
n(X1+...+Xn)]
=1
naE[X1]+...+E[Xn]a
=
n𝜇n
=𝜇
E[X¯]=E[1n(X1+...+Xn)]=1n[E[X1]+...+E[Xn]]=n𝜇n=𝜇
Var(⏨X)=Var(1
n(X1+...+Xn))
=1
n2Var(X1+...+Xn)
=1
n2n∑i=1Var(Xi)
=n𝜎2
n2
=𝜎2
n
Var(X¯)=Var(1n(X1+...+Xn))=1n2Var(X1+...+Xn)=1n2∑i=1nVar(Xi)=n𝜎2n2=𝜎2n Note on simplification• If two random variables XX and YY are independent, then Var(X+Y)=Var(X)+Var(Y)Var(X+Y)=Var(X)+Var(Y).• Since X1,...,XnX1,...,Xn are i.i.d, XiXi and XjXj are pairwise independent for all i,j∈{1,...,n}i,j∈{1,...,n} and i≠ji≠j The standard deviation of ⏨XX¯ is 𝜎/n𝜎/n
